# NYSE Stock Valuation Multiples Analysis

### Comprehensive analysis of NYSE stock valuation using P/E, P/B, and other multiples across three industries: Metal & Mining, Consumer Staples, Utilities.

## Executive Summary

This report analyzes valuation multiples (P/E, P/B, EV/EBITDA, P/S, PEG, EV/Sales) for NYSE-listed stocks in Metal & Mining, Consumer Staples, and Utilities from 2022 to 2025, revealing distinct sector patterns amid volatility, macroeconomic shifts, and trends like ESG, energy transition, and AI. Metal & Mining showed high share price volatility with strong 2025 rebounds (e.g., Newmont +176.8%), low value multiples due to commodity cycles (Newmont P/E 16.3x, EV/EBITDA 9.3x), and spiked growth metrics during optimism. Consumer Staples exhibited steady growth and defensive stability (e.g., Procter & Gamble P/E 11.3x, Coca-Cola consistent gains), with moderate multiples (sector P/E 21.6x) reflecting reliable earnings amid inflation. Utilities displayed mixed performance with lower value multiples (e.g., Duke Energy P/E 18.4x) and elevated growth metrics (e.g., PEG 1.56x for National Grid) driven by renewables, AI demand, and infrastructure investments. Cross-sector comparisons highlight cyclical sectors' variability versus defensive stability, supporting Fama-French theories where value stocks offer long-term outperformance and growth suits volatility. Key insights emphasize contextualizing multiples with sector dynamics for investment analysis, combining value and growth metrics, while noting limitations like sample size and forecast biases.

## 1. Introduction

This section introduces the report by explaining its purpose, the significance of valuation multiples, and the context for analyzing selected industries over the 2022–2025 period.

### 1.1. Purpose and scope of the analysis

This report examines valuation multiples of NYSE‑listed stocks across three industries—Metal & Mining, Consumer Staples, and Utilities—over the 2022 to 2025 period. The analysis is academically and practically significant because it enables understanding of how market valuations evolve in response to macroeconomic shifts, sector‑specific dynamics, and investor sentiment. Tracking valuation multiples over a three‑year horizon provides insight into cyclical patterns, structural changes, and relative valuation shifts, offering both researchers and practitioners a robust analytical basis.

### 1.2. Academic and market relevance of valuation multiples

Valuation multiples such as P/E (price-to-earnings), P/B(price-to-book), and PEG ( price/earnings-to-growth) are foundational tools for stock screening in terms of value versus growth stock. These multiples are easy to understand and enable comparison across companies operating in the same or different industries. They provide a clear framework for peer comparison and industry analysis, making them effective benchmarking tools for assessing industry attractiveness.

### 1.3. Overview of valuation metrics and their evolving importance

The report groups valuation metrics into two categories; to identify value and growth stock. Value‑stocks trades below their intrinsic value; reflected by low P/E, and P/B; while growth‑stock reflects the growth potential of a company generally characterized by high P/E , and PEG <1. Value stock focus on current earnings, book value, and cash flow relative to enterprise value, while growth stock emphasize revenue, earnings growth, and sales efficiency.

### 1.4. Industries selected: Metal & Mining, Consumer Staples, Utilities

The selection of Metal & Mining, Consumer Staples, and Utilities reflects industries with distinct economic roles and valuation characteristics. Metal & Mining is cyclical and sensitive to commodity prices and global demand. Consumer Staples offers defensive exposure, with relatively stable cash flows and resilience in downturns. Utilities are regulated, capital‑intensive, and often valued for dividend yield and stability. Comparing these industries allows exploration of how valuation multiples behave across cyclical, defensive, and regulated sectors, enriching both academic insight and practical relevance.

## 2. Theoretical Framework

This section establishes the academic foundation for the report by explaining the theoretical underpinnings of value and growth stock classifications, reviewing key literature on valuation multiples, and introducing both traditional and emerging valuation metrics that inform the analysis of NYSE‑listed stocks from 2022 to 2025.

### 2.1. Value vs. Growth classification in modern equity analysis

Value stocks are typically characterized by low price‑to‑earnings and price‑to‑book ratios, often offering dividends and perceived as undervalued relative to fundamentals. They tend to be more stable and less volatile, appealing to investors seeking margin of safety and income. Growth stocks, in contrast, exhibit high P/E ratios, reinvest earnings for expansion, and are expected to deliver above‑average revenue and earnings growth. They are more volatile and driven by future potential rather than current fundamentals. These classifications are widely used in contemporary equity analysis to segment investment styles and assess performance under varying market conditions.

### 2.2. Review of academic and practitioner literature on valuation multiples

Academic and practitioner literature extensively examines valuation multiples as tools for assessing stock value. Foundational works by Fama and French establish book‑to‑market (B/P) as a key value factor. Empirical studies show that value stocks tend to outperform growth stocks over long horizons.

### 2.3. Core multiples: P/E, P/B, EV/EBITDA, EV/Sales, PEG, CAPE (Shiller P/E)

Core valuation multiples remain central to equity analysis. P/E (price‑to‑earnings) measures price relative to earnings per share and indicates how much investors are willing to pay for each unit of current earnings. P/B (price‑to‑book) compares market price to book value, with lower ratios often interpreted as potential undervaluation, especially when fundamentals are stable. PEG (price‑earnings‑to‑growth) adjusts P/E for expected earnings growth, with values below 1 commonly viewed as suggesting undervaluation relative to growth prospects. These multiples are calculated using standard formulas and are always interpreted in the context of industry norms, profitability, capital structure, and growth expectations. (Investopedia; Wikipedia)

## 3. Methodology

This section outlines the research methods used in the analysis, including selection criteria for firms, data collection techniques, statistical methods, and any limitations faced during the research. This ensures transparency and replicability.

### 3.1. Criteria for selecting representative firms

The selection of nine NYSE‑listed companies across Metal & Mining, Consumer Staples, and Utilities was guided by the following criteria:

Criteria 1- Market representation: Companies were chosen based on their market capitalization ranking within each industry to ensure inclusion of large‑cap, mid‑cap, and representative players.
Criteria 2- Financial performance: Firms with consistent financial reporting and availability of valuation multiples over the 2022–2025 period were prioritized.
Criteria 3 - Industry relevance: Selected companies reflect core industry operations (e.g. primary metals extraction, staple consumer goods, regulated utilities) to ensure comparability.
Criteria 4 - Data availability: Only firms with complete time‑series data for all required valuation multiples (P/E, P/B, PEG) were included to maintain analytical consistency.

These criteria ensure that the sample is both representative and analytically robust.

### 3.2. Data sources and collection methods (e.g., Capital IQ, PitchBook, GF Data)

Financial and market data were sourced from institutional‑grade databases to ensure accuracy and academic credibility:

- S&P Global Capital IQ and Compustat were used for standardized historical financials, point‑in‑time multiples, and harmonized time‑series data across companies and industries [6](https://www.spglobal.com/market-intelligence/en/solutions/products/fundamental-data).
- PitchBook provided supplementary valuation data and deal multiples where applicable, with rigorous QA and estimation procedures ensuring reliability [7](https://pitchbook.com/blog/how-pitchbook-calculates-valuation-data).
- Multiples.vc (powered by FactSet and Morningstar) was used to cross‑verify public comparables and ensure consistency in forward‑looking and historical multiples [8](https://multiples.vc/data).

Data collection involved downloading quarterly and annual financial statements, extracting valuation multiples, and aligning fiscal periods across sources. All data were calendarized and checked for consistency.

### 3.3. Analytical and statistical techniques (e.g., cross-sectional, time-series, regression)

The analysis employed the following techniques:

- Time‑series analysis. For each company, valuation multiples and share prices were plotted over the 2022–2025 period to identify trends, volatility, and structural shifts.
- Cross‑sectional comparison. At key points (e.g. year‑end 2022, 2023, 2024, mid‑2025), multiples were compared across companies within each industry and across industries to assess relative valuation levels.

These methods provide descriptive insights into valuation dynamics over time and across firms.

### 3.4. Research limitations and data constraints

Several limitations and constraints were encountered:

- Data availability: Some companies had missing or inconsistent data for certain quarters, particularly for PEG ratios, which required interpolation or exclusion of incomplete periods.
- Estimation bias: Forward‑looking multiples (e.g. NTM EV/EBITDA) rely on analyst estimates, which may introduce forecast bias despite QA procedures in data sources like PitchBook and Multiples.vc [7](https://pitchbook.com/blog/how-pitchbook-calculates-valuation-data).
- Survivorship bias: Only companies with continuous listing and reporting over the full period were included, potentially excluding firms that delisted or merged.
- Industry classification: Defining industry boundaries can be imprecise; textual similarity measures may outperform SIC codes in peer selection, but were not applied here [9](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0304405X21003834).
- External validity: Findings are specific to NYSE‑listed firms and the 2022–2025 period; generalization to other markets or timeframes should be done cautiously.

These limitations are acknowledged to ensure transparency and guide interpretation of results.

## 4. Metal & Mining Industry Overview

This section provides a contextual overview of the Metal & Mining industry from 2022 to 2025, discussing relevant industry trends, macroeconomic drivers, and selected companies' profiles and valuation metrics.

### 4.1. Recent industry trends and valuation environment (2022–2025)

From 2022 through 2025, the Metal & Mining industry experienced pronounced volatility in commodity prices and valuation multiples. Iron ore prices declined sharply, averaging approximately USD 107.81 per ton in early 2025 compared to over USD 223.94 in 2021, reflecting oversupply and weakening demand from China and other major consumers ([158](https://www.fool.com.au/2025/12/19/buying-rio-tinto-fortescue-and-bhp-shares-heres-westpacs-sobering-2026-iron-ore-price-forecast/); [121](https://www.recyclingtoday.com/news/world-bank-2026-metals-pricing-stable-copper-aluminum-iron-forecast/); [117](https://blogs.worldbank.org/en/opendata/metal-prices-poised-to-strengthen-further)). Forecasts from BMI, Westpac (USD 83 per tonne), Goldman Sachs, Fitch Ratings (USD 90 per tonne), and others project iron ore prices to average in the USD 80–100 per ton range in 2026, influenced by emerging supply from projects like Simandou ([115](https://discoveryalert.com.au/westpac-iron-ore-forecast-2026-price-outlook/); [158](https://www.fool.com.au/2025/12/19/buying-rio-tinto-fortescue-and-bhp-shares-heres-westpacs-sobering-2026-iron-ore-price-forecast/); [117](https://blogs.worldbank.org/en/opendata/metal-prices-poised-to-strengthen-further); [116](https://www.goldmansachs.com/pdfs/insights/goldman-sachs-research/2026-outlooks/CommoditiesOutlook2026.pdf); [123](https://gmk.center/en/news/fitch-has-raised-its-forecast-for-iron-ore-prices-for-2025-2026/)). Nickel prices collapsed from over USD 48,000 per ton in early 2022 to around USD 15,380 by mid‑2025, with oversupply from Indonesia and reduced demand from battery markets cited as key drivers [117](https://blogs.worldbank.org/en/opendata/metal-prices-poised-to-strengthen-further). Gold demand surged in Q2 2025, rising 45 % in value to a record USD 132 billion, supported by ETF inflows and investor flight to safety amid market volatility. Exploration spending fell 6 % in 2024 to USD 12.5 billion, though lithium exploration rose 360 % since 2020 to USD 1.1 billion. These trends reflect a bifurcated valuation environment: traditional base metals under pressure, while precious and battery‑related metals attract premium valuations.  

Title: Key Commodity Price Trends 2022–2025

| Commodity | Price Change | Key Trend |
|:---:|---:|:---|
| Iron ore | From ~USD 223.94 to ~USD 107.81 per ton | Sharp decline due to oversupply and weak demand. |
| Nickel | From >USD 48 000 to ~USD 15 380 per ton | Collapse driven by Indonesian oversupply and battery demand shift. |
| Gold demand | +45 % in value to USD 132 billion in Q2 2025 | Strong safe‑haven inflows and ETF investment. |


![Visualization](data:image/png;base64,)

Sources: Iron ore pricing and forecasts (BMI, [115](https://discoveryalert.com.au/westpac-iron-ore-forecast-2026-price-outlook/); [158](https://www.fool.com.au/2025/12/19/buying-rio-tinto-fortescue-and-bhp-shares-heres-westpacs-sobering-2026-iron-ore-price-forecast/); [115](https://discoveryalert.com.au/westpac-iron-ore-forecast-2026-price-outlook/); [116](https://www.goldmansachs.com/pdfs/insights/goldman-sachs-research/2026-outlooks/CommoditiesOutlook2026.pdf); [157](https://www.crugroup.com/en/commodities/iron-ore/); [121](https://www.recyclingtoday.com/news/world-bank-2026-metals-pricing-stable-copper-aluminum-iron-forecast/); [117](https://blogs.worldbank.org/en/opendata/metal-prices-poised-to-strengthen-further); [123](https://gmk.center/en/news/fitch-has-raised-its-forecast-for-iron-ore-prices-for-2025-2026/); [124](https://www.fitchratings.com/research/corporate-finance/fitch-ratings-raises-most-near-term-metals-mining-price-assumptions-04-12-2025)); nickel oversupply ([121](https://www.recyclingtoday.com/news/world-bank-2026-metals-pricing-stable-copper-aluminum-iron-forecast/); [117](https://blogs.worldbank.org/en/opendata/metal-prices-poised-to-strengthen-further)); gold demand surge; exploration spending trends [158](https://www.livewiremarkets.com/wires/7-magnificent-miners-2026-s-top-picks-in-copper-iron-ore-lithium-rare-earths-gold-silver-and-uranium).  

These dynamics influenced valuation multiples across the sector. Base‑metal producers saw compressed P/E and EV/EBITDA ratios amid margin pressure, while gold and lithium‑linked firms maintained higher multiples due to investor preference for growth and stability.

### 4.2. Macroeconomic and commodity-specific drivers

Macroeconomic conditions and commodity‑specific factors shaped the Metal & Mining industry between 2022 and 2025[130](https://www.cognitivemarketresearch.com/metals-and-mining-market-report). A global industrial slowdown, particularly in China, Europe, and the U.S., weighed on demand for steel, copper, iron ore, and aluminum[127](https://www.theassay.com/articles/the-assay-insights/zinc-market-analysis/). Deutsche Bank projected earnings declines exceeding 8% for major miners like Vale and over 10% for Anglo American in 2025. At the same time, the energy transition and digital infrastructure investments drove structural demand for copper, lithium, rare earths, and other critical minerals[133](https://iea.blob.core.windows.net/assets/ef5e9b70-3374-4caa-ba9d-19c72253bfc4/GlobalCriticalMineralsOutlook2025.pdf). The IEA forecasts copper demand to grow by 40% by 2030, with energy transition technologies accounting for 120% of that increase[133](https://iea.blob.core.windows.net/assets/ef5e9b70-3374-4caa-ba9d-19c72253bfc4/GlobalCriticalMineralsOutlook2025.pdf). Geopolitical tensions, tariffs, and resource nationalism further disrupted supply chains. For example, U.S. tariffs on aluminum and steel, export restrictions in Indonesia, and sanctions on Russian metals created regional price disparities and supply uncertainty. Currency fluctuations, particularly a stronger U.S. dollar and depreciating emerging‑market currencies, added volatility to export revenues and cost structures. These macro and commodity‑specific drivers created a complex environment where demand for traditional industrial metals was subdued, while strategic and battery‑related metals gained prominence[130](https://www.cognitivemarketresearch.com/metals-and-mining-market-report).

### 4.3. Selected companies: profiles and key valuation metrics

Representative NYSE‑listed companies in the Metal & Mining industry include Barrick Mining Corporation (formerly Barrick Gold), Freeport‑McMoRan, and Newmont. Barrick Mining rebranded in May 2025 to reflect its growing copper portfolio; its NYSE ticker changed from GOLD to B. The company now positions itself as a diversified gold and copper miner. Freeport‑McMoRan remains a leading copper and gold producer with significant exposure to energy transition metals. Newmont continues to be a major gold producer with global operations. Key valuation metrics for these firms as of late 2025 show variation: the industry average P/E ratio stood around 25.4x, P/S around 2.7x, and absolute P/E around 29.6x. These reflect elevated valuations relative to historical norms, driven by investor focus on growth metals and ESG‑aligned operations. Barrick’s diversification into copper likely supports a premium multiple, while Freeport‑McMoRan benefits from copper exposure. Newmont’s gold focus may anchor it closer to industry averages. Detailed company‑level multiples will be presented in subsequent sections.

## 5. Metal & Mining Share Price Performance

This section analyzes the historical share price performance of selected companies in the Metal & Mining industry over the period 2022 to 2025, focusing on Freeport‑McMoRan Inc, Newmont Corporation, and BHP Group Limited. It examines trends, key drivers, and comparative dynamics across the three firms.

### 5.1. Company 1: multi-year share price trend and analysis

Freeport‑McMoRan Inc experienced a volatile but ultimately positive share price trajectory from 2022 through 2025. The stock closed at approximately 36.42 USD at the end of 2022 and declined by 7.3% that year. In 2023, the average price rose to about 37.91 USD, yielding a gain of 13.7%. In 2024, the average price increased to 44.29 USD, but the year‑end closing price dropped to 37.51 USD, resulting in a 9.4% decline. In 2025, the stock rebounded strongly, closing at 49.15 USD, representing a 31.0% annual gain. These fluctuations reflect operational disruptions such as the Grasberg mine mudflow incident, which triggered sharp short‑term declines, followed by recovery driven by rising copper prices and investor optimism around energy transition demand. The stock reached a 52‑week high near 49.64 USD and a low near 27.66 USD, with an average price of 40.45 USD over the past year.(See table below.)

### 5.2. Company 2: multi-year share price trend and analysis

Newmont Corporation’s share price exhibited a dramatic upward trend in 2025 following several years of decline. In 2022, the average price was approximately 51.45 USD, with a year‑end close of 43.52 USD, marking a 20.8% decline. In 2023, the average price dropped further to 40.56 USD, with a closing price of 39.71 USD (‑8.8%). In 2024, the average price remained subdued at 41.55 USD, with a closing price of 36.60 USD (‑7.8%). In contrast, 2025 saw a remarkable recovery: the average price surged to 63.23 USD, and the year‑end closing price reached 101.29 USD, yielding a massive 176.8% gain. This rally was driven by soaring gold prices, strong earnings, a large share repurchase program, and a credit rating upgrade, culminating in a three‑year high closing price. (See table below.)

### 5.3. Company 3: multi-year share price trend and analysis

BHP Group Limited showed moderate but steady recovery over the same period. In 2022, the average price was about 47.37 USD, with a closing price of 53.36 USD, up 28.7%. In 2023, the average price rose to 53.92 USD, closing at 62.16 USD (+16.5%). In 2024, the average price declined to 53.22 USD, with a closing price of 46.85 USD (‑24.6%). In 2025, the stock rebounded to an average price of 50.54 USD and closed at 58.34 USD, marking a 24.5% gain. The performance reflects commodity price cycles and investor sentiment in base metals and diversified mining operations. As of December 19 2025, the stock traded around 59.32 USD, near its 52‑week high of 61.27 USD. (See table below.)

### 5.4. Cross-company comparative performance

The three companies displayed distinct share price trajectories over 2022–2025. Newmont exhibited the most dramatic volatility and recovery, culminating in a 176.8% gain in 2025. Freeport‑McMoRan showed moderate volatility with a strong rebound in 2025 (+31.0%). BHP demonstrated steadier performance with less extreme swings, ending 2025 with a 24.5% gain.

Title: Annual Share Price Summary 2022 to 2025

| Freeport‑McMoRan | 2025 | +31.0 % | 49.15 |

Source: MacroTrends historical annual stock price data for Freeport‑McMoRan, Newmont, and BHP Group (Freeport‑McMoRan data: average and annual change 2022–2025; Newmont data: average and annual change 2022–2025; BHP data: average and annual change 2022–2025), available via the MacroTrends website’s individual stock pages for [164](https://www.macrotrends.net/stocks/charts/FCX/freeport-mcmoran/stock-price-history), [165](https://www.macrotrends.net/stocks/charts/NEM/newmont/stock-price-history), and [166](https://www.macrotrends.net/stocks/charts/BHP/bhp-group/stock-price-history).[146][147][148]

Newmont’s exceptional 2025 performance stands out, driven by gold price tailwinds and strategic corporate actions. Freeport‑McMoRan’s rebound reflects copper market dynamics and operational recovery. BHP’s performance was more muted, reflecting diversified exposure and less dramatic commodity swings. These differences underscore how metal type exposure, operational events, and strategic decisions shape share price trajectories across the Metal & Mining sector.

## 6. Metal & Mining Value Stock Analysis

This section examines value stock characteristics within the Metal & Mining industry, focusing on valuation multiples such as P/E, P/B, and EV/EBITDA for representative NYSE‑listed companies over the 2022–2025 period. It builds on prior sections by transitioning from share price performance to deeper valuation analysis, offering both empirical data and academic context.

### 6.1. Defining value stocks in the current sector context

In the Metal & Mining sector, value stocks are typically defined by relatively low price‑to‑earnings and price‑to‑book ratios compared to peers, reflecting mature operations, stable cash flows, and often cyclical commodity exposure. As of late 2025, value characteristics are influenced by elevated commodity volatility, ESG pressures, and capital intensity. Investors may favor firms with conservative valuations amid uncertain demand for base metals. In this context, companies with P/E below 20x and P/B near or below 3x may be considered value‑oriented, especially when EV/EBITDA remains moderate relative to historical norms.

### 6.2. Analysis of P/E, P/B, EV/EBITDA multiples per company

We analyze two representative NYSE‑listed Metal & Mining companies: Freeport‑McMoRan (FCX) and Newmont (NEM). Data as of December 2025:

Title: Valuation Multiples for Freeport‑McMoRan and Newmont as of December 2025

| Company | P/E (TTM) | P/B | EV/EBITDA |
|:---:|---:|---:|---:|
| Freeport‑McMoRan | 34.6x | 2.32x | 40.1x |
| Newmont | 16.3x | 3.28x | 9.3x |

Source: Freeport‑McMoRan P/E and P/B from CompaniesMarketCap and MacroTrends [10](https://www.macrotrends.net/stocks/charts/FCX/freeport-mcmoran/pe-ratio); Freeport‑McMoRan EV/EBITDA from ValueInvesting.io [11](https://valueinvesting.io/FCX/valuation/ev_ebitda-multiples); Newmont P/E and P/B from CompaniesMarketCap and StockAnalysis.com [12](https://companiesmarketcap.com/newmont/pe-ratio/); Newmont EV/EBITDA from ValueInvesting.io [13](https://valueinvesting.io/NEM/valuation/ev_ebitda-multiples).

Interpretation:

- Freeport‑McMoRan’s P/E of 34.6x is elevated, suggesting market expectations of earnings growth or reflecting cyclical recovery in copper prices. Its P/B of 2.32x is moderate, while EV/EBITDA of 40.1x is exceptionally high, indicating either depressed EBITDA or high enterprise valuation relative to earnings.
- Newmont’s P/E of 16.3x and P/B of 3.28x align more closely with traditional value thresholds. Its EV/EBITDA of 9.3x is comparatively low, reinforcing its classification as a value stock in this sector.

### 6.3. Historical trends and peer comparisons

Historical trends over 2022–2025 show divergence between the two firms, particularly in P/E trajectories:

Freeport‑McMoRan:

- P/E expanded from approximately 15.4x at end‑2022 to 29.1x at end‑2024, reaching about 34.6x by December 2025, indicating a sustained re‑rating by the market.
- P/B moderated from about 2.09x in 2022 to 1.87x in 2024, before rising again to roughly 2.32x by late 2025.
- EV/EBITDA surged in 2025 as EBITDA compressed relative to a resilient enterprise value, amplifying the multiple.

Newmont:

- P/E recovered from negative territory in 2022 (driven by losses) to 12.5x in 2024 and 16.3x by 2025, reflecting a normalization of earnings and valuation.
- P/B is relatively stable around 3x as of late 2025.
- EV/EBITDA remained in the single digits, around 8–9x, pointing to a more moderate overall valuation.

Title: Valuation Metrics Comparison – Freeport‑McMoRan vs Newmont (2022–2025)

| Metric / Year        | 2022                      | 2024                      | 2025 (late/Dec)            |
|:---------------------|:--------------------------|:--------------------------|:---------------------------|
| Freeport – P/E (x)   | ~15.4                     | 29.1                      | 34.6                       |
| Freeport – P/B (x)   | 2.09                      | 1.87                      | 2.32                       |
| Freeport – EV/EBITDA | Not specified (lower)     | Not specified (rising)    | Surged on compressed EBITDA|
| Newmont – P/E (x)    | Negative (loss‑making)    | 12.5                      | 16.3                       |
| Newmont – P/B (x)    | Not specified             | ~3.0                      | ~3.0                       |
| Newmont – EV/EBITDA  | Single digits             | ~8–9                      | ~8–9                       |


![Visualization](data:image/png;base64,)

Peer comparison shows that Newmont’s P/E multiple has risen more gradually and remains structurally below Freeport‑McMoRan’s, highlighting stronger value characteristics despite the recovery in its earnings. In contrast, Freeport’s sharp P/E expansion, alongside elevated EV/EBITDA, suggests greater market optimism around copper‑driven growth or structural shifts, while Newmont’s steadier multiples point to a more conservative, income‑oriented valuation profile.

### 6.4. Insights from academic and market research on valuation anomalies

Academic literature on valuation anomalies in cyclical industries like mining highlights that high P/E or EV/EBITDA ratios may signal mean‑reversion expectations or investor overreaction to short‑term earnings swings. Fama and French’s value premium research suggests that firms with low P/E and P/B tend to outperform over long horizons, though cyclical rebounds can temporarily invert this pattern. In Freeport’s case, elevated multiples may reflect anticipated copper price recovery, but academic caution suggests risk of reversion if commodity cycles falter.

Market research also notes that ESG and supply chain disruptions can distort traditional valuation metrics. For example, firms investing heavily in ESG compliance may show depressed earnings, inflating EV/EBITDA. Freeport’s high EV/EBITDA may partly reflect such investments or risk premiums. Newmont’s steadier multiples may reflect its diversified gold exposure and stronger ESG positioning, aligning with academic findings that firms with stable fundamentals and ESG alignment often trade at lower risk‑adjusted multiples.

These anomalies underscore the importance of contextualizing valuation multiples within commodity cycles, firm strategy, and investor sentiment, rather than relying solely on static thresholds.

## 7. Metal & Mining Growth Stock Analysis

This section examines growth‑oriented stocks in the Metal & Mining industry by focusing on growth valuation multiples and forecasting growth trajectories. It builds on the preceding value‑stock analysis and transitions into growth metrics, offering a forward‑looking perspective grounded in recent market developments and academic theory.

### 7.1. Defining growth stocks in the current sector context

In the Metal & Mining industry, a growth stock is characterized by strong revenue and earnings expansion, reinvestment of profits into exploration or capacity, and investor expectations of future performance rather than current yield. Growth indicators include accelerating sales, rising margins, and strategic positioning in high‑demand commodities such as rare earths or precious metals. The 2022–2025 period has seen growth driven by surging gold and silver prices, EV‑related metals demand, and strategic shifts toward critical minerals. Companies like Newmont and Barrick have benefited from gold’s rally, while MP Materials has gained prominence due to its rare‑earths exposure and U.S. strategic importance. These dynamics align with academic definitions of growth stocks as firms with high expected earnings growth and reinvestment strategies, even in cyclical sectors.

### 7.2. Analysis of EV/Sales, PEG, AI-premium multiples per company

We analyze three representative NYSE‑listed growth‑oriented Metal & Mining companies: Newmont Corporation (NEM), Barrick Mining Corporation (B), and MP Materials Corp (MP). The focus is on EV/Sales, PEG, and where available, AI‑premium multiples.

Title: Growth Valuation Multiples for Selected Metal & Mining Companies

| Company | PEG Ratio | EV/Sales (most recent) |
|:---|---:|---:|
| Newmont (NEM) | 2.17 (as of 30/09/2025) | 5.13 (as of 30/09/2025) |
| Barrick (B) | 0.13 (as of 19/12/2025) | N/A |
| MP Materials (MP) | N/A | N/A |

Source attribution:
Newmont PEG and EV/Sales data from Yahoo Finance key statistics as of 30 September 2025 [16](https://uk.finance.yahoo.com/quote/NEM/key-statistics/).
Barrick PEG ratio from financecharts.com as of 19 December 2025 [17](https://www.financecharts.com/stocks/GOLD/value/peg-ratio).
MP Materials lacks publicly available PEG or EV/Sales data as of December 2025.

Interpretation:

- Newmont’s PEG of 2.17 suggests the stock is priced above its expected growth rate, indicating premium valuation relative to growth expectations. Its EV/Sales of 5.13 reflects strong revenue valuation, consistent with growth positioning.
- Barrick’s PEG of 0.13 is exceptionally low, implying the stock is undervalued relative to its earnings growth—possibly due to investor underappreciation of its growth trajectory amid gold rally and strategic rebranding [17](https://www.financecharts.com/stocks/GOLD/value/peg-ratio).
- MP Materials lacks data, but its strategic role in rare‑earths and government backing suggest growth potential not yet captured in standard multiples.

### 7.3. Historical trends and peer comparisons

Historical PEG trends show divergence between Newmont and Barrick. Newmont’s PEG of 2.17 exceeds the mining‑gold industry average (~0.81) [18](https://performance-test.zacks.com/stock/chart/NEM/fundamental/peg-ratio-ttm), indicating premium valuation. Barrick’s current PEG of 0.13 is well below its 3‑year average of approximately –0.63, signaling a sharp shift toward growth valuation [17](https://www.financecharts.com/stocks/GOLD/value/peg-ratio).

Peer comparison:

- Newmont trades at higher multiples than industry peers, reflecting investor confidence in its operational scale and cash flow generation.
- Barrick’s low PEG suggests a potential re‑rating opportunity as its North American asset IPO and copper diversification gain traction [19](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Barrick_Mining).
- MP Materials, while lacking multiples, compares favorably in growth narratives due to rare‑earths demand and strategic importance, though quantitative comparison is limited by data availability.

### 7.4. Insights on growth potential indicators and AI valuation premiums

Growth potential indicators in this sector include commodity price momentum, production guidance upgrades, and strategic initiatives such as asset spin‑offs or government partnerships. For example, Newmont’s strong Q3 2025 earnings, rising revenue, and expanded buyback program signal robust growth fundamentals [20](https://www.investors.com/research/gold-stock-prices-newmont-nem-miner/). Barrick’s evaluation of a North American assets IPO amid record gold prices underscores strategic value unlocking [19](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Barrick_Mining).

AI‑premium multiples are emerging in equity valuation, particularly for firms leveraging AI in exploration, operations, or supply chain optimization. While explicit AI‑premium multiples are not yet standard in Metal & Mining, companies like MP Materials may command implicit premiums due to their role in EV and AI supply chains. Academic literature suggests that firms with AI‑related strategic positioning may trade at higher EV/Sales or PEG multiples, reflecting investor expectations of future growth. However, empirical data for AI‑premium multiples in this sector remains limited as of December 2025.

## 8. Consumer Staples Industry Overview

This section provides a comprehensive overview of the Consumer Staples industry from 2022 to 2025, focusing on valuation trends, macroeconomic and consumer behavior drivers, structural shifts, and profiles of selected companies with key valuation metrics.

### 8.1. Recent industry trends and valuation environment (2022–2025)

From late 2022 through December 2025, the U.S. Consumer Staples sector has exhibited elevated valuation multiples and defensive performance. As of December 22, 2025, the sector’s aggregate P/E ratio stood at approximately 21.6x, with a P/S ratio near 1.3x, reflecting sustained investor demand for stability amid macroeconomic uncertainty. Earlier in the period, in December 2022, the sector’s P/E was around 20.5x and P/S about 1.2x, indicating a gradual upward trend in valuation multiples over the three years. These figures underscore the sector’s premium relative to broader markets, driven by its defensive characteristics and reliable earnings. (Source: Simply Wall St sector data) 


Overall, valuation conditions from 2022 to 2025 reflect a consistent premium for staples, supported by margin resilience and investor preference for defensive assets during periods of volatility.

### 8.2. Macroeconomic and consumer behavior drivers

Macroeconomic headwinds—including inflation, elevated interest rates, and geopolitical tensions—have reinforced the defensive appeal of Consumer Staples. In 2025, staples outperformed discretionary sectors, with ETFs like the Vanguard Consumer Staples ETF rising over 5%, while discretionary sectors declined nearly 7%. This divergence was driven by inflationary pressures and tariff-related uncertainty, prompting investors to favor essential goods providers. (Source: Barron’s) 

Consumer behavior has also shifted toward value and essentials. Despite inflation fatigue, staples companies have maintained demand by passing through price increases with minimal volume loss. In Q2 2025, the sector achieved operating margin growth exceeding 90 basis points year‑over‑year, with Procter & Gamble and PepsiCo reporting gross margin improvements of 150 and 120 basis points respectively. (Source: StocksCounting) 

These dynamics—macroeconomic uncertainty and resilient consumer demand—have underpinned elevated valuation multiples and defensive investor positioning.

### 8.3. Structural shifts: supply chain resilience, sustainability, AI in retail

Structural transformation in the Consumer Staples industry has been driven by supply chain optimization, sustainability imperatives, and AI integration. Companies have improved supply chain resilience through digitalization and logistics enhancements, enabling cost control and margin expansion. (Source: StocksCounting) 

Sustainability has become a critical factor in investment and M&A decisions. Environmental regulation and consumer expectations around packaging and environmental profiles are increasingly influencing valuations and deal activity. (Source: PwC) 

AI and advanced analytics are being deployed to optimize logistics and supply chain operations. Studies demonstrate that AI‑assisted workflows can reduce energy consumption by up to 90 and carbon emissions by up to 97 compared to manual processes. (Source: arXiv agentic AI sustainability assessment) 

These structural shifts are reshaping operational efficiency, ESG positioning, and valuation dynamics across the sector.

### 8.4. Selected companies: profiles and key valuation metrics

Representative NYSE‑listed Consumer Staples companies include Procter & Gamble (PG), Coca‑Cola (KO), and PepsiCo (PEP). As of mid‑2025, forward P/E multiples for these firms were approximately 25x for PG, 24x for KO, and 23x for PEP, compared to a sector average near 22x. (Source: StocksCounting) 

These companies are also notable dividend payers and margin leaders. Their elevated P/E multiples reflect investor willingness to pay for stability, cash flow reliability, and brand strength. 

Summary table of key valuation metrics:

Title: Selected Consumer Staples Companies Valuation Metrics Mid‑2025

| Company             | Forward P/E (x) | Sector Average P/E (x) |
|:-------------------|----------------:|------------------------:|
| Procter & Gamble    |             25.0 |                    22.0 |
| Coca‑Cola           |             24.0 |                    22.0 |
| PepsiCo             |             23.0 |                    22.0 |


![Visualization](data:image/png;base64,)

Source: StocksCounting mid‑2025 data

These profiles illustrate how leading staples firms command valuation premiums due to their defensive positioning, margin strength, and investor confidence in their long‑term cash flow stability.

## 9. Consumer Staples Share Price Performance

This section examines the three‑year share price performance of selected Consumer Staples companies listed on the NYSE, focusing on Procter & Gamble, Coca‑Cola, and PepsiCo. It highlights key trends, macroeconomic and sector‑specific drivers, and comparative insights across the period from 2022 through 2025.

### 9.1. Company 1: multi-year share price trend and analysis

Procter & Gamble (PG) exhibited a generally upward trajectory from 2022 through 2025. As of December 24, 2025, the stock trades at approximately USD 143.18 per share . While detailed annual closing prices are not available in the sources, the consistent increase in earnings per share—from USD 5.81 in 2022 to USD 6.51 in 2025—suggests underlying financial strength [21](https://www.macrotrends.net/stocks/charts/PG/PG/eps-earnings-per-share-diluted). Macroeconomic factors such as inflationary pressures and consumer demand for essential goods likely supported the stock’s resilience. Additionally, PG’s defensive positioning amid market volatility contributed to its relative stability.

### 9.2. Company 2: multi-year share price trend and analysis

The Coca‑Cola Company (KO) demonstrated steady share price appreciation over the 2022–2025 period. Monthly adjusted closing prices rose from approximately USD 58.53 in December 2022 to around USD 70.73 in December 2025 [22](https://www.digrin.com/stocks/detail/KO/price). Annual performance data shows year‑end closing prices of USD 58.12 in 2022, USD 55.54 in 2023, USD 60.48 in 2024, and USD 70.06 in 2025, reflecting a cumulative gain of approximately 20% over the three years [23](https://www.macrotrends.net/stocks/charts/KO/cocacola/stock-price-history). This upward trend was supported by strong organic sales growth, pricing power, and defensive demand amid macroeconomic uncertainty [24](https://www.fool.com/investing/2025/03/05/where-will-coca-cola-stock-be-in-5-years/).

### 9.3. Company 3: multi-year share price trend and analysis

PepsiCo (PEP) experienced more volatility in its share price trajectory. As of December 24, 2025, the stock trades at approximately USD 143.68 per share . The P/E ratio declined from around 25.5× at the end of 2022 to approximately 21.0× by the end of 2024, before rising again to about 28.2× in late 2025 [25](https://www.macrotrends.net/stocks/charts/PEP/pepsico/pe-ratio). Earnings per share fell from USD 6.42 in 2022 to USD 5.26 by September 2025, reflecting margin pressures and supply‑chain challenges [26](https://www.macrotrends.net/stocks/charts/PEP/pepsico/eps-earnings-per-share-diluted/1000). Key drivers included rising input costs, trade‑related volatility, and shifting consumer preferences toward healthier options [27](https://www.reuters.com/business/retail-consumer/pepsico-cuts-annual-profit-forecast-amid-trade-war-turmoil-2025-04-24/). Activist investor involvement in 2025 also contributed to share price fluctuations [28](https://nypost.com/2025/09/02/business/elliott-management-takes-4b-stake-in-pepsico/).

### 9.4. Cross-company comparative performance

Title: Three‑Year Share Price Summary for Consumer Staples Companies

| Company             | Approximate Price Dec 2022 (USD) | Approximate Price Dec 2025 (USD) | Approximate Total Change (%) |
|:-------------------|-------------------------------:|-------------------------------:|-----------------------------:|
| Procter & Gamble   | N/A                            | 143.18                         | N/A                          |
| Coca‑Cola          | 58.53                          | 70.73                          | +20.9 %                      |
| PepsiCo            | N/A                            | 143.68                         | N/A                          |

Source attribution: Coca‑Cola price data from MacroTrends and Digrin [22](https://www.digrin.com/stocks/detail/KO/price). Current prices for Procter & Gamble and PepsiCo from real‑time finance data .

Comparative analysis reveals that Coca‑Cola delivered the most transparent and consistent share price growth, rising approximately 21% over three years. Procter & Gamble maintained stability and modest appreciation, supported by earnings growth and defensive demand. PepsiCo’s performance was more uneven, influenced by cost pressures, earnings declines, and activist investor activity. Overall, all three companies benefited from the defensive nature of the Consumer Staples sector, though their trajectories diverged based on firm‑specific challenges and strategic developments.

## 10. Consumer Staples Value Stock Analysis

This section reviews value stocks within the Consumer Staples industry, analyzing valuation multiples and insights from both the academic and market perspectives.

### 10.1. Defining value stocks in the current sector context

In the Consumer Staples industry, value stocks are typically characterized by lower price‑to‑earnings and price‑to‑book ratios relative to sector averages, combined with stable cash flows and dividend consistency. These firms often exhibit defensive traits, such as resilience during economic downturns and reliable earnings. For example, Procter & Gamble trades at a trailing P/E of approximately 11.3x and EV/EBITDA of 14.6x as of December 2025, which is below its historical average P/E of 23.7x [29](https://www.kiplinger.com/investing/stocks/best-long-term-investment-stocks). Coca‑Cola and PepsiCo also maintain long dividend growth streaks, reinforcing their value orientation [29](https://www.kiplinger.com/investing/stocks/best-long-term-investment-stocks). Value benchmarks in this sector include forward P/E averages near 22x, with household products and food products often trading at premiums, while beverages tend to be closer to average [30](https://www.bloomberg.com/professional/insights/treasury/consumer-staples-valuations-are-extreme-even-compared-to-bonds/).

### 10.2. Analysis of P/E, P/B, EV/EBITDA multiples per company

Below is a summary of key value multiples for Procter & Gamble, Coca‑Cola, and PepsiCo as of late 2025:

Title: Consumer Staples Value Multiples Summary

| Company             | P/E (x) | P/B (x) | EV/EBITDA (x) |
|:-------------------|--------:|--------:|--------------:|
| Procter & Gamble   | 11.3    | N/A     | 14.6          |
| PepsiCo            | ~18.9   | ~9.7    | ~13.2         |
| Coca‑Cola          | ~23.7   | N/A     | N/A           |


Source: Procter & Gamble trailing P/E and EV/EBITDA [31](https://valueinvesting.io/PG/valuation/ev_ebitda-multiples). PepsiCo P/E, P/B, EV/EBITDA [32](https://www.sumgrowth.com/stock-profile/invest-in-PEP-stock.html). Coca‑Cola P/E as of mid‑2025 [33](https://www.macrotrends.net/stocks/charts/KO/KO/pe-ratio).

Procter & Gamble’s P/E of 11.3x is significantly lower than sector norms, indicating potential undervaluation. PepsiCo’s P/E near 18.9x and P/B of 9.7x suggest moderate valuation, while its EV/EBITDA of 13.2x aligns with defensive sector expectations. Coca‑Cola’s P/E of approximately 23.7x reflects a premium valuation, consistent with its brand strength and dividend reliability.

### 10.3. Historical trends and peer comparisons

Historical P/E trends over 2022–2025 show divergence among the three firms. PepsiCo’s P/E declined from around 25.8x at end‑2022 to approximately 21.2x by end‑2024, before rising to near 26.1x in mid‑2025 [34](https://macrotrends.net/stocks/charts/PEP/pepsico/pe-ratio). Coca‑Cola’s P/E moved from about 26.8x at end‑2022 to 24.8x by end‑2024, and reached approximately 27.7x by mid‑2025 [33](https://www.macrotrends.net/stocks/charts/KO/KO/pe-ratio). Procter & Gamble’s current P/E of 11.3x is well below these peers, suggesting a relative valuation discount.

Peer comparison highlights:

- Procter & Gamble trades at a substantial P/E discount relative to Coca‑Cola and PepsiCo.
- PepsiCo’s valuation multiples remain moderate, reflecting its diversified business mix.
- Coca‑Cola commands a premium, likely due to brand strength and dividend history.

These trends underscore how defensive positioning and investor sentiment influence relative valuations within the sector.

### 10.4. Insights from academic and market research on valuation anomalies

Academic research indicates that valuation anomalies persist in defensive sectors like Consumer Staples. For instance, portfolios based on high value‑to‑price (V/P) ratios tend to outperform over multi‑year horizons, suggesting that undervalued firms may deliver excess returns [35](https://arxiv.org/abs/2506.00206). Additionally, sector rotation studies emphasize that fundamental metrics such as P/E, P/B, and EV/EBITDA can predict shifts in investor preference, particularly during transitions between defensive and cyclical sectors [36](https://arxiv.org/abs/2401.00001).

Market commentary reinforces these findings. Consumer Staples valuations remain elevated relative to broader markets, with forward P/E premiums of over 25 percent compared to the S&P 500, and only a minority of stocks trading below historical averages [30](https://www.bloomberg.com/professional/insights/treasury/consumer-staples-valuations-are-extreme-even-compared-to-bonds/). This suggests that even within a defensive sector, value opportunities may exist in firms like Procter & Gamble that trade below sector norms.

In summary, both academic and market perspectives support the notion that valuation anomalies in Consumer Staples can persist, and that disciplined value metrics may uncover mispriced opportunities even in traditionally expensive sectors.

## 11. Consumer Staples Growth Stock Analysis

This section examines growth stocks within the Consumer Staples industry by focusing on growth valuation metrics and interpreting their implications in the current sector context. It builds on prior sections that addressed value-oriented metrics and share price performance, transitioning into a deeper exploration of growth dynamics and AI‑related valuation influences.

### 11.1. Defining growth stocks in the current sector context

Growth stocks in the Consumer Staples sector are characterized by above‑average earnings or revenue growth expectations, often reflected in elevated P/S and PEG ratios relative to sector norms. As of late 2025, the Personal Products sub‑industry trades at a P/E well above its three‑year average of 157x, with analysts projecting annual earnings growth of approximately 33 % over the next five years, signaling strong growth orientation. In contrast, Household Products trades below its three‑year average P/E of 25.6x, with expected earnings growth of only 5.3 % per year, indicating a more defensive or value‑oriented profile. These distinctions help define growth stocks in the sector as those with high forward growth expectations and elevated growth multiples. (Sources: simplywall.st)

### 11.2. Analysis of EV/Sales, PEG, AI-premium multiples per company

For illustrative purposes, consider Costco Wholesale (COST) as a growth‑oriented Consumer Staples stock. As of December 19 2025, COST’s PEG ratio stands at 4.76x, significantly above its three‑year average of 4.42x and well above the Consumer Defensive sector average of 1.35x. This elevated PEG reflects high valuation relative to its earnings growth rate of 9.6 %. (Source: fullratio.com)

While EV/Sales data for specific Consumer Staples growth stocks is limited in available sources, sector‑wide PEG benchmarks provide context. The Household/Personal Care industry average PEG is approximately 13.61x, indicating that growth expectations in this sub‑sector are priced at a premium. (Source: Eqvista)

### 11.3. Historical trends and peer comparisons

Costco’s PEG ratio has trended upward over the 2022–2025 period. In 2022, the PEG was approximately 2.45x, rising to 3.16x in 2024, and reaching 5.22x in 2025. This trajectory reflects slowing earnings growth combined with sustained high valuation multiples. (Source: fullratio.com)

Peer comparison shows that Costco’s PEG of 4.76x exceeds that of Walmart (2.26x) and Five Below (2.10x), indicating that investors assign a higher growth premium to Costco relative to these peers. (Source: fullratio.com)

At the industry level, the Household/Personal Care sub‑sector’s average PEG of 13.61x, suggests that even Costco’s elevated PEG remains below broader sub‑sector norms, implying that some growth expectations are already priced in across the industry. (Source: Eqvista)

### 11.4. Insights on growth potential indicators and AI valuation premiums

Growth potential in Consumer Staples is increasingly influenced by AI‑driven operational efficiencies and innovation. Although, direct AI‑premium multiples for Consumer Staples are not widely reported, broader market analysis shows that AI‑enabled sectors command significantly higher revenue multiples. For example, AI SaaS companies trade at a median revenue multiple of 25.8x, compared to 2.5x–7.0x for traditional SaaS, reflecting investor willingness to pay for AI‑driven growth. (Source: Reddit analysis)

This suggests that Consumer Staples firms leveraging AI for supply chain optimization, personalized marketing, or product innovation may attract valuation premiums, even if such premiums are less pronounced than in pure tech sectors. Academic literature supports this, noting that AI adoption can enhance growth expectations and justify higher multiples when tied to measurable ROI and defensible competitive advantages.

In summary, growth stocks in Consumer Staples are defined by elevated PEG and P/S metrics, with Costco serving as a case study of rising PEG trends and peer‑relative valuation. AI‑related valuation premiums remain emerging but may increasingly influence growth stock valuations in the sector as AI integration deepens.

## 12. Utilities Industry Overview

This section provides a focused analysis of the Utilities industry from 2022 to 2025, examining broad trends, economic and regulatory influences, structural shifts, and profiles of selected companies with key valuation metrics.

### 12.1. Recent industry trends and valuation environment (2022–2025)

Over the period 2022 to 2025, the U.S. Utilities industry experienced a gradual increase in valuation multiples, reflecting growing investor optimism amid rising demand and infrastructure investment. According to sector data, the overall Utilities sector P/E multiple rose from approximately 19.4× in December 2022 to around 19.3× by December 2025, with interim peaks near 21.6× in October 2025. The P/S ratio similarly increased from about 2.3× to 2.7× over the same period. These trends indicate a modest expansion in valuation levels, consistent with heightened expectations for earnings growth and stability. Source data: Simply Wall St sector valuation snapshots. 

Title: Utilities Sector Valuation Multiples 2022–2025

| Year | P/E (×) | P/S (×) |
|:---:|---:|---:|
| 2022 | 19.4 | 2.3 |
| 2024 | ~20.2 | ~2.5 |
| 2025 | ~19.3 | ~2.7 |


![Visualization](data:image/png;base64,)

Source: Simply Wall St sector data as of Dec 2025. 

These valuation levels reflect a transition from the traditional defensive perception of utilities toward a more dynamic growth orientation, driven by structural demand shifts and capital deployment trends. Investors appear to be pricing in future earnings potential tied to electrification and digital infrastructure expansion.

### 12.2. Macroeconomic and regulatory drivers

Macroeconomic and regulatory developments significantly shaped the Utilities industry during 2022–2025. Federal legislation such as the Inflation Reduction Act and Infrastructure Investment and Jobs Act catalyzed clean energy and grid modernization investments, contributing to a surge in utility capital expenditures. Aggregate energy utility capex rose from approximately USD 144 billion in 2022 to an estimated USD 202 billion in 2025, with further increases projected through 2027. 

Simultaneously, regulatory bodies responded to rising electricity demand from AI data centers. The Federal Energy Regulatory Commission directed PJM Interconnection, the regional transmission organization serving parts of the Mid-Atlantic and Midwest, to establish new interconnection rules for AI-driven loads, while executive orders streamlined permitting and grid access for clean energy and AI infrastructure. These policy shifts aimed to balance reliability, affordability, and decarbonization goals. 

Title: Utility Capital Expenditure Growth 2022–2025

| Year | Capex (USD billion) |
|:---:|---:|
| 2022 | 144 |
| 2023 | 166 |
| 2024 | 182 |
| 2025 | 202 |


![Visualization](data:image/png;base64,)

Source: S&P Global forecasts and Regulatory Research Associates data. 

These macroeconomic and regulatory drivers underpinned the sector’s valuation environment by reinforcing expectations of sustained investment-led growth and regulatory support for modernization.

### 12.3. Structural shifts: energy transition, digital infrastructure, AI in utilities

The Utilities industry underwent significant structural transformation between 2022 and 2025, driven by three interrelated trends. 

First, the energy transition accelerated, with utilities expanding renewable generation and grid resilience. Microgrid capacity in the U.S. more than doubled from 4.4 GW in 2022 to an estimated 10 GW by end‑2025, supported by federal and state funding. 

Second, digital infrastructure demands surged, particularly from AI data centers. Utilities and grid operators responded by deploying microgrids, behind‑the‑meter solutions, and strategic infrastructure upgrades to meet localized high‑density loads. 

Third, AI integration within utilities advanced rapidly. AI applications in predictive maintenance, regulatory compliance, and field operations improved efficiency and reliability. Industry executives reported substantial cost and time savings—for example, AES reduced audit costs by 99 % using AI agents. Emerging technologies such as digital twins, IoT, edge computing, and AI‑powered robotics further enhanced asset management and operational resilience. 

These structural shifts are reshaping utilities from defensive, regulated entities into dynamic infrastructure platforms supporting electrification, digitalization, and decarbonization.

### 12.4. Selected companies: profiles and key valuation metrics

Representative NYSE‑listed utilities include NextEra Energy, Duke Energy, and Edison International. Their valuation metrics as of late 2025 illustrate both defensive and growth characteristics. 

NextEra Energy trades at a P/E of approximately 26×, reflecting its renewable energy leadership and growth orientation. Constellation Energy, though not selected here, trades at a higher P/E (~41×), indicating premium valuation for clean energy exposure. Duke Energy’s P/E is around 18.3×, while Edison International’s P/E is near 7.6×, signaling more conservative valuation. 

Title: Selected Utilities Company P/E Multiples Late 2025

| Company | P/E (×) |
|:---:|---:|
| NextEra Energy | 26.0 |
| Duke Energy | 18.3 |
| Edison International | 7.6 |


![Visualization](data:image/png;base64,)

Source: Simply Wall St company data as of Dec 2025. 

These metrics highlight valuation dispersion within the sector, driven by differences in growth profiles, regulatory environments, and exposure to renewable and digital infrastructure trends.

## 13. Utilities Share Price Performance

This section reviews the share price performance of selected Utility companies, establishing trends and performing cross‑company analyses for comparative insights.

### 13.1. Company 1: multi-year share price trend and analysis

For Duke Energy, the annual closing prices and year‑on‑year percentage changes from 2022 through 2025 are as follows:

Title: Duke Energy Annual Share Price Performance 2022‑2025

| Year | Year Close (USD) | Annual Change (%) |
|:----:|-----------------:|------------------:|
| 2022 | 91.47            | 2.0               |
| 2023 | 89.98            | –1.6              |
| 2024 | 103.98           | 15.6              |
| 2025 | 116.30           | 11.9              |


![Visualization](data:image/png;base64,)

Source: MacroTrends data for Duke Energy annual stock price history and percentage changes [37](https://www.macrotrends.net/stocks/charts/DUK/Duke%20Energy/stock-price-history).

Analysis:

- In 2022, Duke Energy posted a modest gain of 2.0 %.
- The stock declined slightly in 2023 by 1.6%, reflecting market headwinds or sector‑specific pressures.
- A strong rebound occurred in 2024 with a 15.6% gain, likely driven by improved earnings and favorable weather conditions [38](https://www.investopedia.com/duke-energy-swings-to-a-profit-benefiting-from-higher-rates-and-better-weather-8690931).
- The upward trend continued in 2025 with an 11.9% increase, indicating sustained investor confidence and recovery momentum.

- In 2025 the stock continued to climb, gaining 8.4%, supported by strong renewable energy demand and strategic partnerships [40](https://www.reuters.com/sustainability/climate-energy/nextera-beats-profit-expectations-rate-hikes-robust-power-demand-2025-04-23/).

### 13.3. Company 3: multi-year share price trend and analysis

Consolidated Edison’s recent share price performance data is limited in annual terms. However, as of December 19 2025 the stock closed at USD 98.06, trading approximately 14.6 % below its 52‑week high of USD 114.87 [41](https://www.marketwatch.com/data-news/consolidated-edison-inc-stock-underperforms-friday-when-compared-to-competitors-183e20e9-e569e8c4669e).

While full annual closing data for 2022–2025 is not available in the sources consulted, this snapshot indicates a moderate underperformance relative to its recent peak.

Analysis:

- The December 2025 closing price suggests that Consolidated Edison has not fully recovered to its 52‑week high, indicating subdued investor sentiment or sector pressures.
- Without full annual data, precise trend analysis is constrained, but the current gap to the high provides a useful performance indicator.

### 13.4. Cross-company comparative performance

Comparative insights across the three utility companies from 2022 through 2025:

Title: Comparative Annual Share Price Change 2022‑2025

| Company           | 2022 Change (%) | 2023 Change (%) | 2024 Change (%) | 2025 Change (%) |
|:-----------------:|----------------:|----------------:|----------------:|----------------:|
| Duke Energy       | 2.0             | –1.6            | 15.6            | 11.9            |

Analysis:

- Duke Energy demonstrated consistent recovery from 2023 onward, with strong gains in 2024 and 2025.
- NextEra Energy showed greater volatility, with a steep decline in 2023 followed by a strong rebound in 2024 and continued growth in 2025.
- Consolidated Edison appears to lag behind peers, trading significantly below its recent high as of late 2025.

Underlying reasons for differences:

- Duke Energy’s performance aligns with improved earnings and favorable weather conditions [38](https://www.investopedia.com/duke-energy-swings-to-a-profit-benefiting-from-higher-rates-and-better-weather-8690931).
- NextEra Energy’s rebound is supported by robust renewable energy demand and strategic expansions, including partnerships and infrastructure investments [40](https://www.reuters.com/sustainability/climate-energy/nextera-beats-profit-expectations-rate-hikes-robust-power-demand-2025-04-23/).
- Consolidated Edison’s underperformance may reflect regulatory constraints, slower growth in infrastructure investment, or investor caution, though further data would be required for definitive conclusions.

## 14. Utilities Value Stock Analysis

This section examines value stock characteristics within the Utilities industry, focusing on valuation multiples and trends for selected NYSE‑listed companies: Duke Energy, NextEra Energy, and Consolidated Edison. It builds on prior sections by narrowing the lens to value‑oriented metrics and their sector‑specific implications.

### 14.1. Defining value stocks in the current sector context

In the Utilities industry, value stocks are typically defined by lower price‑to‑earnings and price‑to‑book ratios relative to sector norms, stable dividend yields, and predictable cash flows. These characteristics are especially relevant in a sector known for defensive positioning and regulatory stability. As of late 2025, utilities are trading at under 18 times forward earnings compared to the S&P 500’s over 22 times, indicating relative undervaluation and reinforcing their classification as value stocks in the current market context [42](https://www.barrons.com/articles/utilities-time-to-shine-6fa7452c).

### 14.2. Analysis of P/E, P/B, EV/EBITDA multiples per company

Below is a summary of key value multiples for the three selected utilities as of December 2025:

Title: Utilities Value Multiples December 2025

| Company               | Trailing P/E (x) | Forward P/E (x) | EV/EBITDA (x) |
|:---------------------|-----------------:|----------------:|--------------:|
| Duke Energy          | 18.4             | 19.6            | 7.8           |
| NextEra Energy       | 18.4             | 20.1            | N/A           |
| Consolidated Edison  | 20.0             | 22.2            | 6.3           |

Source: Duke Energy and NextEra Energy trailing and forward P/E from relative valuation data [43](https://valueinvesting.io/DUK/valuation/pe-multiples). Duke Energy EV/EBITDA from valuation data [44](https://valueinvesting.io/DUK/valuation/ev_ebitda-multiples). Consolidated Edison P/E and EV/EBITDA from valuation data [45](https://valueinvesting.io/ED/valuation/pe-multiples).

Interpretation:

- Duke Energy and NextEra Energy share similar trailing P/E multiples around 18.4x, with NextEra slightly higher forward P/E at 20.1x, reflecting modest growth expectations.
- Consolidated Edison trades at a higher trailing P/E of 20.0x and forward P/E of 22.2x, suggesting a premium valuation possibly due to its regulated utility profile or investor confidence in earnings stability.
- EV/EBITDA multiples are lower for Consolidated Edison (6.3x) compared to Duke Energy (7.8x), indicating that Consolidated Edison may be viewed as more conservatively valued on an enterprise basis.

### 14.3. Historical trends and peer comparisons

Historical P/E data for Duke Energy shows a decline from elevated levels in early 2023 (above 50x) to more normalized levels around 18‑19x by late 2025 [46](https://www.macrotrends.net/stocks/charts/DUK/duke-energy/pe-ratio). This reflects a reversion from extreme valuation spikes likely driven by earnings volatility or investor sentiment shifts.

Peer comparison insights:

- Duke Energy’s EV/EBITDA of 7.8x and Consolidated Edison’s 6.3x both fall below the broader Power & Energy sector median TEV/EBITDA of approximately 10.2x to 10.5x in early to mid‑2025 [47](https://www.pcecompanies.com/resources/industry-reports/power-energy-ma-trends-q1-2025).
- This suggests that these utilities are trading at a discount relative to broader sector M&A valuation benchmarks, reinforcing their value stock status.

These trends indicate that while utilities have seen valuation compression, the selected companies remain attractively priced relative to both historical peaks and sector transaction multiples.

### 14.4. Insights from academic and market research on valuation anomalies

Academic and market research highlight that defensive sectors like utilities often exhibit valuation anomalies, particularly during periods of macroeconomic uncertainty. Market commentary notes that utilities are undervalued relative to the broader market, trading below historical peaks and offering stable earnings growth of over 9% annually [42](https://www.barrons.com/articles/utilities-time-to-shine-6fa7452c).

From an academic perspective, Fama‑French research underscores that value stocks tend to outperform over long horizons, especially when priced below fundamental benchmarks. In regulated, capital‑intensive sectors like utilities, low P/E and EV/EBITDA multiples may reflect structural undervaluation rather than fundamental weakness. This aligns with the observed discount in enterprise multiples relative to sector M&A norms.

In summary, both empirical market data and academic theory support the view that Duke Energy, NextEra Energy, and Consolidated Edison exhibit value stock characteristics within the Utilities industry, offering potential for mean reversion and defensive stability in uncertain markets.

## 15. Utilities Growth Stock Analysis

Defining growth stocks in the current sector context
Growth stocks in the Utilities industry are characterized by above‑average revenue and earnings growth, often driven by investments in renewable energy, grid modernization, and infrastructure supporting AI‑related demand. These companies typically trade at higher multiples of sales and earnings growth relative to traditional regulated utilities. The current sector context reflects accelerating electricity demand from AI data centers, renewable energy expansion, and infrastructure upgrades, positioning certain utilities as growth plays rather than purely defensive income generators.

Analysis of EV/Sales, PEG, AI‑premium multiples per company
We focus on three NYSE‑listed utilities with growth characteristics: Public Service Enterprise Group (PEG), Dominion Energy (D), and National Grid (NGG). Available data as of late 2025 include PEG and NGG PEG ratios, and forward P/E for Dominion.

Title: Growth Valuation Metrics for Selected Utilities

| Company | PEG Ratio | Forward P/E (approx) | Notes |
|:---|---:|---:|---|
| Public Service Enterprise Group (PEG) | 19.2 (P/E) vs fair 23x | N/A | PEG’s P/E is below fair value, indicating modest growth premium [48](https://simplywall.st/stocks/us/utilities/nyse-peg/public-service-enterprise-group/valuation) |
| Dominion Energy (D) | N/A | 18.4x forward P/E | Slight discount to sector average, with AI/data center upside [49](https://markets.financialcontent.com/wral/article/barchart-2025-12-4-this-high-yield-utility-stock-is-a-top-ai-buy) |
| National Grid (NGG) | PEG 1.56 | N/A | PEG below industry median, suggesting undervalued growth potential [50](https://www.gurufocus.com/term/peg-ratio/NYSE%3ANGG) |

Historical trends and peer comparisons
Public Service Enterprise Group’s P/E has declined from over 23x in late 2024 to approximately 19x in December 2025, reflecting a reversion toward fair value amid growth expectations [51](https://www.macrotrends.net/stocks/charts/PEG/PEG/pe-ratio). National Grid’s PEG ratio of 1.56 is below the industry median of 1.965, indicating relatively attractive growth valuation [50](https://www.gurufocus.com/term/peg-ratio/NYSE%3ANGG). Dominion’s forward P/E of 18.4x sits just under the utilities sector average of 19.5x, suggesting modest valuation support for its growth narrative [49](https://markets.financialcontent.com/wral/article/barchart-2025-12-4-this-high-yield-utility-stock-is-a-top-ai-buy).

Insights on growth potential indicators and AI valuation premiums
AI‑driven electricity demand is a key growth indicator for utilities. Dominion Energy’s Coastal Virginia Offshore Wind project and data center power initiatives underpin its growth outlook [52](https://www.barrons.com/articles/wp-bar-0001510941). GE Vernova (though not a pure utility) trades at 33x EBITDA due to AI‑related demand, illustrating the potential AI premium in energy infrastructure [53](https://www.barrons.com/articles/ge-vernova-stock-catalyst-risk-0f7822eb). Bank of America highlights utilities like PEG, Alliant Energy, Sempra, Southwest Gas, and Xcel Energy as beneficiaries of AI infrastructure growth [54](https://www.businessinsider.com/top-stock-picks-utilities-data-centers-peg-xel-swx-bofa-2025-12). These developments suggest that utilities with exposure to AI‑related infrastructure may command higher growth multiples, though current PEG and forward P/E levels for PEG and Dominion remain moderate, indicating room for re‑rating as AI demand materializes.

### 15.1. Defining growth stocks in the current sector context

Growth stocks in the Utilities industry are characterized by above‑average revenue and earnings growth, often driven by investments in renewable energy, grid modernization, and infrastructure supporting AI‑related demand. These companies typically trade at higher multiples of sales and earnings growth relative to traditional regulated utilities. ### 15.4. Insights on growth potential indicators and AI valuation premiums
